sentences.text: Yes, when we-- my father carried me out from that-- lager [INAUDIBLE] camp number one and put me on the train.
Now that position, that place where we-- where you boarded the train was 60 kilometers from Dachau.
I didn't know it at that time, but our destination was Dachau.
It took us three days to make those 60-- 60 miles-- 60 kilometers. 60 kilometers that's about--
36, 40, around that, miles, which could have been done in a couple of hours.
Three days and three nights.
So when we moved out a few kilometers-- at night, we didn't move at night.
For we started at dawn.
And then in the morning, we went a few kilometers.
And we stopped by-- by-- by woods.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And next, because the road probably was blocked for whatever reason.
Next to us, stopped the German military train, a military train painted military colors.
So an English surveillance plane came around.
And we all looked up.
And we saw the round insignia of the RAF.
We knew it-- it wasn't German.
We really-- at that time, I really didn't know who it was.
But I knew it wasn't German, so it had to be our friends.
And he disappeared quickly.
And then a squadron of plane came, because they saw the German military train and started to shoot at us.
Many of the-- of the inmates, many of the prisoners were killed at that time.
I remember throwing myself off from the train.
Now, the train wasn't on a level.
It was in such embankment as though it is in Europe and probably in the United States, too, but I've never seen it here.
The train was on a higher embankment.
And so that was a distance.
And how I didn't kill myself, I don't know.
And I run into that woods, or crawled into that woods.
I don't-- at that time, I didn't see him.
But he kept an eye on me.
And I came.
And from that camp-- from in those woods, I saw a bottle with white fluid.
There were a lot of medicines that people took out from the-- from lager one, because there were doctors, many doctors, especially Lithuanian Jewish.
That were in-- this was called the camp where the Lithuanian Jews were kept.
And there were medication.
I don't know where I got it from or whatever.
There was a white bottle with white stuff.
It's going to sound very stupid.
But I opened that bottle, and I drank it, the whole bottle.
This is how thirsty I was.
This is how I didn't care what is going to happen.
And I told it to my father.
And he says, how could you do it?
You're going to kill your-- kill your-- Do you know what it was?
Absolutely nothing.
It could have been-- I mean, the white stuff, what is it called?
Milk of magnesia, it could have been anything, you know.
But I didn't know at that time what it was.
Anyway, when my father came off that train, he had-- his hand was cut by a can of food, you know, the military cans.
He had this can-- he had this can constantly on the train with him.
I don't know where he got it from and what was even inside.
Because nothing at that time mattered to me anymore.
Well, somebody wanted to pull it out from his hand, so they cut his left hand.
By the time we came to Dachau, he had already gangrene.
A number of the people from that train started to run the fields-- into the fields.
But you could see only the head moving and-- and the-- what is it called?
House-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] home-- houseware, or the old people.
The old people were shooting with the guns, you know, the people that were not in the army.
But what are they called?
Homeware?
Self-defense, like the-- the civil defense.
People from civil defense, which were mostly old Germans, they were shooting at the prisoners.
And they killed many.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The same way-- the same ways that some were killed.
And some ran into the-- these huts in the village and were hiding.
Like, I mentioned before, Samuel [INAUDIBLE] described that he saw someone.
He saw an American-- he hid in one of those houses.
And the next day, he saw an American tank with a American soldier.
And then he came out.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
So what happened-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what happened was that some people were hiding in these houses till the Germans-- until the Americans came in.
Because it was within hours, everything has-- has changed.
And to this very day, there is three monuments with Stars of David by that railroad by these woods.
Do you know what kept you going when you were so ill and so starving?
What inside you sustained you?
I-- at that time, nothing mattered.
It was my father, my father that kept me pushing, my father that kept talking to me.
You will survive.
You will see.
You'll come home.
And everything was going to be OK.
He kept giving me life.
He had given me hope.
And I'm sure if I would have been alone, we wouldn't be talking today.
So by the time we came to Dachau, we were both taken to the hospital.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- I remember, it had to be the very next day, that while I was myself they-- they kicked at this-- [INAUDIBLE] had given me had turned into a big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] abscess, cyst with pus in it.
And they had to operate on me.
Was this after liberation?
No, no, this were not doctors that did that.
They were called [INAUDIBLE], you know, not even paramedics.
These were people that had learned those things from others in the camp.
And where I-- my injury was something that didn't need to-- you didn't have to be an expert.
You cut it open.
And if you healed, it healed.
But my father, they amputated the left arm.
And within hours-- because it was all the same day, I remember saying Kaddish later-- this Italian, big guy, tall man, broad shoulders came over to me.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH], your comrade kaput.
Now, it wasn't my comrade.
It was my father.
And he saw that we came in together.
And I remember like in a haze somebody saying Kaddish with me.
And that was two days after liberation or three days.
I'm still confused which day we are liberated.
But I have a certificate of that that I have received from the-- the United Nations organization in Germany.
So I have the-- the date that my father died.
And it's two days after liberation.
And at that day, my whole world ended.
I-- I didn't want to go out from that hospital.
Because without my father, nothing else-- nothing else mattered.
And when we-- finally, they sent me from the hospital into a-- into a-- it's called a block, you know, a barrack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in there, the very first day that I was there, I met a man also crying about his father.
But he didn't know that his father was alive.
He knew he was sick.
And they-- he had angina.
And they separated them.
And later, we became very close friends.
And he became my brother-in-law.
But we were both sitting there and crying like two children.
We were both the same age.
Maybe he was a year older or so.
And one day-- one day.
The same day, the-- the first or the second day, as I'm sitting with him and talking how to figure out what-- what is happening here.
We still didn't fully comprehend, fully comprehend what is going on around us, that we have a life.
Or, are we alive?
Or, does it matter?
Someone that used to work for my grandfather was in the same-- was in the same barrack.
I don't remember exactly how he recognized me or how I recognized him.
Because I don't remember this man.
He said to me, [INAUDIBLE], come to the window.
And he called me over to the window.
And I couldn't believe my eyes what I saw.
An uncle of mine that in Lodz ghetto, the ghetto in Lodz, was was sentenced to death by the criminal police.
And it's going to sound stupid.
But the execution is supposed to be done after the war.
And every selection that was in Auschwitz-- he was in Auschwitz, not in Birkenau.
They put these eight people aside.
And this is how he survived.
And I saw this uncle that I knew was dead.
You know, it's like it says in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
If all waters would become ink, I couldn't describe that moment.
And, you know, I couldn't describe all this moment of our meeting.
We were hugging and crying.
And I wouldn't let him go.
All of a sudden, I have some--
No, my mother's brother.
My mother's brother.
[SIGHS] And we hugged like that for a while.
And then I went back into my-- my barrack.
We stayed for, I don't know, a week or so, two weeks.
And he was sent to Munich, where it was military--
I remember when I was still in the hospital that I saw American soldiers with the red-- with this red-- you know, with the red star.
And I-- I-- like in a haze, I heard some of the survivors yelling some stupid things about welcoming.
You know, I don't remember exactly.
One says, long live America, long live white bread, long live coffee, things like chocolate, something like that.
But all this went by me.
Because at that time when I was in the hospital, life didn't matter.
Life did not matter.
And I-- when I found this uncle, you know, he sort of-- you know, at least was something to hold onto.
Because all these years, I-- I was still this child.
I was a protected child all these years.
And even-- even in the ghetto where I did all these things for my father.
And in the camp, I was my father's child.
It was my father that was the man.
I was the child.
And then we were in Munich.
We were sent to Munich where it was a military-- what's called the SS [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is barracks.
So we stayed there until I heard that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother's brother's wife survived.
And she's in Landsberg.
So I went by foot from Munich to Landsberg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And in Landsberg, we stayed with our aunt for a while till, you know, I met-- I met my wife.
And in a very short time, we wrote an engagement.
Orthodox way, because that's the way she wanted.
And-- and we got married.
We got married in 1946.
April 2, 1946.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, at least that began my life again, you know.
I started to live again.
I had a very devoted wife.
And I kept saying, this is the kind of girl that my parents would want, they want me to marry, you know?
And we-- we did have-- we have a very good life.
We had brought up two children.
And-- and now, when we had the children and the children got married, and-- I'm skipping all those difficulties during the years.

When it came around a holiday, and I was cursing, cursing, why do have-- do-- can do-- have to-- do we have to be alone without having any relatives to share with us a holiday, especially for the young children?
When I saw families, I was never envious or jealous about anything.
But I used to see people having families or talking about their brothers and uncles.
And they have fights with this one and that one.
That is what I was so envious.
I would have given anything.
But this is something that you can't turn back the clock.
This was taken from us in such a brutal way at such a young stage in life-- young age in life.
People are losing parents, you lose, you know, to sickness.
It-- it might be easier for the person that lost it.
But it is so cruel for us that it happened in a way that-- in such an innocent-- innocent way.
I'm sure that our children have complexes in our being children of survivors, like all the children have.
And they do.
Well, I never had thought that my foot will step on the Polish ground ever.
Because when I returned to Poland in 1945 to bring out my aunt-- the uncle that survived, his wife.
He was in no condition to go to Poland.
And I said, I'll do it.
We talked in the street about it.
And I went right away without going back.
I had nothing to take with me, so we just went.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so when I went [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] when I went back to Poland at that time, there was a big sign.
We greet you, our brothers.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Very soon, I learned that I'm not a brother, even though that my father was an officer in the Polish army.
He fought for the independence of the country.
I was a Jew.
And Jews were being thrown off the train and beaten.
That was in '45.
Yeah.
But I didn't look Jewish, you know.
As you'll see from my picture, I looked very gentile, without even realizing it.
I was scared all the time until I got to my aunt.
Well, anyway, I left without my aunt.
I came back to Germany.
I found her.
But she had other plans.
And that's another story.
And we came back to-- to-- Germany.
And but when I-- when I-- you asked me about the factory, the-- my business in Poland.
Originally, I had bought a shop, a clothing factory, in Brooklyn.
How I bought it, how I did it, I don't know.
But I did it.
And we were employing 100 people.
And things started to go well for us till the import started from Eastern Europe.
And that put me out of business.
And if you can't lick them, you have to join them.
I had to make a living.
So the situation came up where we were able to do some business to bring in some garments from Eastern Europe.
It started with Poland, then Hungary, Romania, Bulgaria, Czechoslovakia.
And I did it for 17 years.
Tell me about that incident with the matzah.
I think that [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, there were many incidents in Poland where I came across anti-Semitic statements, where people would say, oh, I didn't mean anything.
We just talk here like that.
I had come across some very, very righteous Poles.
I have heard-- and I had a book about an organization called [INAUDIBLE], that they were purely dedicated to save Jews.
I also know about them playing music, having a merry-go-round, and singing songs at the time when the Warsaw ghetto was burning, saying that it's only Jews that are burning there.
I, in my travels, had different kinds-- all kinds of experiences, positive and negative.
I have been to a Hungarian cathedral where I heard the priest saying in Hungarian-- and I do understand Hungarian-- don't believe the Jew, because the Jew lies.
I-- I have went and gone to Polish masses, to cathedrals, and hear what is being said.
But in individual contacts with people, I became very friendly with one of the directors of one of the-- the main factories that we did business with.
It was good for me to be friendly with him.
I could accomplish anything that I wanted just by a telephone call.
So I was invited to his house every time that I was for dinner.
Never did it come up anything anti-Semitic that I heard this man say.
He knew-- I never hid the fact from everyone that I'm Jewish.
I made a point of it that I was Jewish.
I wore a tie with [INAUDIBLE] written all over the ties, that I wanted people to know.
Because I have heard such comments that used to annoy me.
And I wanted to avoid it for my sake.
So this man knew I-- we were Jewish.
And I have been there every time for dinner, never one single word.
This one time, my wife was in Poland with me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were invited for dinner with my wife.
And over there, without drinking vodka, there is no evening, no social meeting.
So we were on the third half-liter of vodka.
But not my wife, but was me, this director, and his wife.
And out of the blue, he says to me, Mr.
Pierson-- as a matter of fact, he said, Mr.
Felix.
You know, this is, in a way, respectful friendliness.
He said mister, but the first name.
Tell me, is it true that the Jewish people use gentile children as blood for masses?
And if I have just a pinch of alcohol, my cheeks turn red immediately.
So I said to him, of course, that they do.
Look at my red cheeks.
You will see.
You know very well, I said, every time I come for dinner, before I eat anything, you have to give me a glass of gentile children's blood.
And I was very sarcastic, because it annoyed me.
And the wife started to scream at him, how can you say a thing like that?
How can you say-- you know, you will offend Mr.
Pierson.
And he said, no, I have heard this all my life.
And I know that Mr.
Pierson will tell me the truth.
So, of course, we talked about it.
And he said, he's sorry that he said that.
But the fact is he wanted to know.
But I must add to this, you know, he wasn't the only one.
What year was this taking place?
This was taking place about '78, '79.
Now, you have heard about the pogrom in Poland, in [INAUDIBLE] 1946.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they changed the democratic system in Poland, when the communist system was ended, what had to be '89, they had shown a movie, a film, something like they show Shoah that they've been showing about [NON-ENGLISH], about the destruction of the Jews.
So they show it.
And I'm not going to go in all to detail.
But they showed how the people were killed.
There were Polish witnesses describing that the Poles did to the Jews in that pogrom what the Germans did to the Jews in Auschwitz.
Because this man was in Auschwitz.
And he saw what was happening.
And it all started, because two children had played in a-- in a sand ravine.
And but they're pulling-- they wanted to take out sand.
And it-- sand-- like an avalanche, and the sand fell down.
And it covered them with sand, and they disappeared.
So they had assumed that these two children were killed.
Now, who would have killed these children?
The Jews.
So that was the reason for the pogrom.
The point I'm trying to make, at the end of that program, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they are interviewing a priest, a Catholic priest.
And they're asking him, is it true that the Jews are using gentiles' children blood for their ritual needs?
In this case, was to strengthen the weakened people that came back from the concentration camp and from Russia.
Not for maztahs, but to give them injection-- blood injections from these children.
So his answer was years ago, it was a fact.
Today, I don't know.
So this is what is happening to this very day.
We're not talking about 20 years ago, you know.
And, of course, we could talk forever about the-- my fights in Poland, or my reactions in Poland when I came across anti-Semitic-- Can I ask you, just before we end, some questions about life here?
Do you have-- you've got how many grandchildren?
We have not enough, but six.
[LAUGHTER] Six grandchildren.
Well, what I would like to say to them is that, with all their might, they should fight against hate and prejudice.
Because, as we see now, what hate and prejudice can lead to.
We don't have to look far.
We have Oklahoma City and other places.
But it's so important that they should fight it as Jewish people and as an American citizen.
Because this country has been very good to us.

And this country, I bless three times a day, even though there were people here in this country that were against-- against helping our people when the knife was at their throat, when they being-- when they were being destroyed en masse, the thousands daily.
And they were organized from the government's side not to help our people.
So they must fight with everything they have for any-- any hatred and any prejudice.
Because that could lead to everything-- I can't imagine that anything worse could be what-- than what happened to us.
Because many of our survivors wish that they wouldn't have survived.
Because this is how-- how you feel after going through what-- what we did.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know from the-- the actions that the parents have taken with their children [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they're going in the right direction, that they're good people.
My father used to-- told me under my bar mitzvah [NON-ENGLISH], which means to be observant Jew in the house and to be a decent person, human being.
I think they are both, they are Jews and human beings on the street and in the house.
But what I-- what I wanted to add-- what I wanted to add-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was the fact that I have six children, six grandchildren.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I keep thinking if two people like us could have six children, those that were killed wasn't just the number that were killed.
Multiply this by all these six numbers, at least minimum of six.
There were so many more millions killed than the numbers actually imply.
And yet, there are people that are trying to deny it.
I am not worried about these deniers.
I am just worried about the people, our people, and the people of the world should stop-- start fighting prejudice and hatred, prejudice and hatred.
Because like the German priest [INAUDIBLE] said, if you don't do it for your fellow man, pretty soon you'll have to do it for yourself.
But there's no one left to help you.
I thank you for having patience to listen to me.

But as you well know, and I realize it in everything that I said, that it's just the-- the scratch the top of what was-- there are certain things that you can't remember, certain things you don't want to remember.
But the reason I did it, because for a long time I couldn't even talk about it, is simply I can't say all these things to our children.
I can't say all these things to our grandchildren.
And if I said any little thing that I tried to our granddaughter, I'd know that it affected her.
You know, she's very-- kids are very sensitive.
They are good people.
They can't imagine.
If you are good, you cannot imagine that the next person could be so cruel.
So-- and I wouldn't want to hurt my grandchildren.

And so I'm glad that one day when they'll be older, they'll be able to understand and see what happened to their grandparents and do everything possible that something like that shouldn't be repeated again.
